accepted.question_text: In the planar magnetic billiard model, where a particle moves inside a billiard table along Larmor circles determined by a strong constant magnetic field, is every totally integrable magnetic billiard necessarily the circular billiard?

evidence.quote: Magnetic billiards for strong magnetic fields. Magnetic billiards were introduced and studied in [101]. In this model, the billiard ball moves under the influence of a constant magnetic field along Larmor circles (for a magnetic field of magnitudeB, the Larmor circles have radius1/B). The only known example of an integrable magnetic billiard is a circular billiard. An algebraic approach to this problem was discussed in [30], [32]. Numerical experiments were performed in [5]. Moreover, the approach of total integrability works well for convex magnetic billiard in aweakconstant magnetic field [28]. Question 1.Is it true that the only totally integrable magnetic billiard in a strong constant magnetic field is necessarily circular?

accepted.question_text: Let N be a smooth convex curve in R^2, and consider the Minkowski billiard in the unit ball of the norm N, defined by the generating function L(s0, s1) = N(gamma(s1) - gamma(s0)) with gamma parameterized by N-arc length. If this Minkowski billiard is totally integrable, must the norm N be Euclidean?

evidence.quote: Avidan, Karasev, and Ostrover [19] on Mahler’s conjecture. Consider a smooth convex curve γ⊂R 2, and a Minkowski (not necessarily symmetric) normN. The Minkowski billiard in γwith respect to the normNis defined as a twist map that corresponds to the generating function L(s0, s1) =N(γ(s 1)−γ(s 0)), and the corresponding variational principleL{s n}= P n L(sn, sn+1),whereγis parametrized byN-arc length parameter (meaning,N( ˙γ(s)) = 1). Thus, the Birkhoff billiard is a special case of the Minkowski billiard whenNis chosen to be a Euclidean norm. Question 2.(Communicated by Yaron Ostrover)Suppose that the Minkowski billiard, corresponding to the normN, in a unit ball ofNis totally integrable. Does this imply that the normNis necessarily Euclidean?

accepted.question_text: Consider Newton's equation u'' = -V'_u(u,t) with V periodic in both u and t. Besides the special case V = V(nu + mt), for which the system is known to be totally integrable near infinity, are there other potential functions V for which the system is totally integrable at infinity?

evidence.quote: Newton’s equation¨u=−V ′ u(u, t)is the Euler-Lagrange equation associated with the La- grangianL(u,˙u, t) = 1 2 ˙u2 −V(u, t). Assume thatVis periodic both inuand int. The corresponding Hamiltonian flow acts onT∗S1 ×S 1. One can prove that this flow is totally integrable on the whole phase space if and only ifVdoes not depend onu. Moreover, if V=V(nu+mt), withm̸= 0, n∈Z, then there exists an integral of motion that is quadratic in momentump, and hence the system is totally integrable in a neighborhood of infinity, |p|> c, for sufficiently largec. Question 3.Are there other potential functionsVsuch that the system is totally integrable at infinity?

accepted.question_text: Consider the elliptic equation Delta u = -V'_u(u, x1, ..., xn), where V is 1-periodic in u, x1, ..., xn. Suppose that for every rotation vector alpha in R^n there exists a foliation of T^(n+1) by graphs of minimal solutions of slope alpha. Does it follow that V does not depend on u?

evidence.quote: Consider an elliptic PDE∆u=−V ′ u(u, x1, . . . , xn), where∆ = Pn i=1 ∂2 xi andVis a 1- periodic function ofu, x 1, . . . , xn. This equation is the Euler-Lagrange equation for the functional R� 1 2 |∇u|2 −V(u, x 1, . . . , xn)  dx1 . . . dxn. Aubry-Mather theory, as well as KAM theory for this equation, were developed by J. Moser [92] and V. Bangert [22]. IfVdoes not depend onu, we have the equation∆u= 0. In this case, for any rotation vectorα∈R n, there exists a family of minimal solutions of the formu=α·x+c. Thus, for everyα, the graphs of these solutions form a foliation ofTn+1(u, x1, . . . , xn)by minimals (hyperplanes of slopeα). Question 4.LetVbe a 1-periodic potential. Suppose that for everyα∈R n there exists a foliation ofT n+1 by graphs of minimal solutions of slopeα. Is it true that, in this case,V does not depend onu?

accepted.question_text: Let (N, g) be an orientable closed hyperbolic surface. Is the Hofer-Zehnder capacity of the unit disk cotangent bundle (D^*N, d lambda) finite?

evidence.quote: Hence, it is enough to prove finiteness for the unit disk cotangent bundleD∗N:=D ∗ 1N. Question: Let(N, g)be an orientable closed hyperbolic surface. Doesc HZ � D∗N, dλ  <∞hold?

A positive answer would, to the best of the author’s knowledge, give the first example of a

accepted.question_text: For a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism on a closed symplectic manifold, is it true that either the number of periodic orbits is finite or the number P(T) of primitive periodic orbits of period at most T grows at least quadratically, in the sense that lim inf_{T -> +infinity} log P(T) / log T >= 2?

evidence.quote: The confirmation of Conjecture 1 is, however,notthe problem proposed here. Instead, we would like to propose the problem of investigating whether the same phenomenon holds in any dimension. Problem 1.Is it true that for a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism on a closed symplectic manifold, either the number of periodic orbits is finite, or periodic orbits grow at least quadratically in the sense thatlim infT→+∞ logP(T) logT ≥2?

accepted.question_text: Does the quadratic lower bound lim inf_{T -> +infinity} log P(T) / log T >= 2 hold for every Hamiltonian diffeomorphism on a standard symplectic torus? More generally, does it hold on every aspherical closed symplectic manifold?

evidence.quote: Problem 2.Is it true thatlim inf T→+∞ logP(T) logT ≥2holds for every Hamiltonian diffeo- morphism on a standard symplectic torus? Or in an arbitrary aspherical closed symplectic manifold?

accepted.question_text: If a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism of CP^n has infinitely many periodic orbits, must those periodic orbits satisfy the quadratic growth bound lim inf_{T -> +infinity} log P(T) / log T >= 2?

evidence.quote: Problem 3.Is it true that for a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism onCPn with infinitely many periodic orbits the inequalitylim infT→+∞ logP(T) logT ≥2holds?

accepted.question_text: Find physically interesting infinite-dimensional billiard systems, ideally arising from equations of mathematical physics such as fluid dynamics or other Euler-Arnold type geodesic equations.

evidence.quote: 1. Find physically interesting examples of infinite-dimensional billiards Motivation:It is a well-known folklore (made into a theorem under certain conditions) that billiard and geodesic flows are closely related by taking appropriate limits, see e.g. [51, 84]. There are various physical problems leading to the construction of geodesics on infinite- dimensional spaces. One of the sources is the Euler-Arnold equation describing geodesics on infinite-dimensional groups. In particular, solutions of the Euler equation of incompressible hydrodynamics can be regarded as geodesics on the group of volume-preserving diffeomor- phisms with respect to the right-invariantL2-metric on the group. A similar framework exists for the KdV equation, magnetohydrodynamics, Heisenberg chain, etc. [14, 15] A natural question is whether there exist interesting infinite-dimensional billiards, possibly related to equations of mathematical physics.

accepted.question_text: Do there exist nontrivial integrable pensive billiards, meaning pensive billiard systems with a nonzero delay function for which one can exhibit explicit choices of boundary curve and delay that yield integrable dynamics?

evidence.quote: 2. Are there nontrivial examples of integrable pensive billiards? Motivation:In [45], a new discrete dynamical system generalizing classical billiards was introduced. Given a planar domain with a boundary curveγand a delay functionℓ(α), the pensive billiard mapis a composition of the classical billiard map with the translation along 6 ===== PAGE 7 ===== Open problems in Billiards and Quantitative Symplectic Geometry the boundary by the distanceℓ(α), where this translation may depend on the incidence angleα; see Figure 1. Figure 1: Pensive Billiard. One of the systems that admits such a description is a puck (or coin) billiard proposed by M. Bialy in [29]: geodesics on a surface obtained by gluing two copies of a given domainDto the cylinder[0, h]×∂Dcan be identified with pensive billiard trajectories for an appropriate delay function. Another example arises in studying 2-dimensional ideal hydrodynamics as a motion of vortex dipoles hitting the boundary of a fluid domain at a certain angle: they split and travel along the boundary in opposite directions until they meet again and merge into a traveling dipole, reflecting from the boundary at the same angle. While the classical billiard, corresponding to the vanishing delay functionℓ(α)≡0, has ellipses as examples of integrable dynamical systems, no interesting integrable cases are known for nontrivial delay. (There are, of course, easy examples of any delay functionℓon a circleγor the delayℓ(α)≡(perimeter)/2on the ellipse.) It was recently proved in [25] that for sufficiently tall puck billiards (i.e., for sufficiently largeh), there are no caustics, and hence no integrability is possible in that case. The question is to find if there exist integrable pensive billiards and present some nontrivial examples by choosing appropriate curvesγ=∂Dand delaysℓon them.

accepted.question_text: Construct a continuous theory for particles with positive and negative mass, or equivalently for billiards in pseudo-Euclidean metrics, and determine whether the resulting Euler-like equations for a fluid of negative density exhibit interesting phenomena.

evidence.quote: 3. Is there a fluid of negative density? Motivation:A mild reformulation of the question above is as follows:Study continuous versions for the interactions of particles of positive and negative mass. Explore the relation

corresponding theory and applications to gravitons are described in [18]. The problem is to construct a continuous version of this theory of particles of negative mass. Are there some interesting phenomena related to the corresponding Euler-like equations for the “fluid of negative density”?

accepted.question_text: Characterize which fiber-wise convex domains in T^*S^n arise as billiard domains W_T associated with convex billiard tables T in R^(n+1).

evidence.quote: Agustin Moreno The set of oriented linesLinR n+1 is a symplectic manifold(L, ω), symplectomorphic to (T ∗Sn, ωstd). Given a convex billiard tableT⊂R n+1, the inducedbilliard domainis WT ={l∈ L:l∩T̸=∅} ⊂ L, which carries the symplectic formω T :=ω| WT.(W T , ωT )is symplectomorphic (via the identificationL ∼= T ∗Sn) to a fiber-wise convex domain inT∗Sn, and therefore diffeomorphic toD ∗Sn. The associatedbilliard mapis fT : (WT , ωT )→(W T , ωT ), which maps a line intersectingTto its billiard reflection, as determined byT. This map preservesthesymplecticformω T. Moreover, whileHamiltonian(andsmooth)intheinterior, fT is only continuous along the boundary∂WT. We call such a mapC0-Hamiltonian. The following are open problems: 1. Characterize the fiber-wise convex domains inT∗Sn which arise from billiard tables. 2. Characterize theC 0-Hamiltonian mapsf:W→Wof a fiber-wise convex domain

accepted.question_text: Characterize which C^0-Hamiltonian self-maps of a fiber-wise convex domain W in T^*S^n arise as billiard maps of convex billiard tables.

evidence.quote: 2. Characterize theC 0-Hamiltonian mapsf:W→Wof a fiber-wise convex domain Wwhich arise as billiard maps of convex billiard tables. To the best of the author’s knowledge, even the casen= 1is open. Moreover, in the latter

accepted.question_text: Let M be a closed manifold that is not a K(pi,1), and let Sigma be a closed connected hypersurface in T^*M that is a regular energy level of a Hamiltonian H: T^*M -> R. Is there always some epsilon > 0 such that almost all energy levels of H in the interval (c - epsilon, c + epsilon) carry a contractible periodic orbit of H?

evidence.quote: Problem statementLetMbe a closed manifold that is not aK(π,1), and endow the cotangent bundleT ∗Mwith its standard symplectic form. We conjecture that, for any closed and connected hypersurfaceΣ⊂T ∗Mand any HamiltonianH:T ∗M→Rthat admitsΣas a regular energy levelΣ =H −1(c), there existsε >0such that almost all energy levels ofHin the interval(c−ε, c+ε)carry a contractible periodic orbit ofH.

accepted.question_text: Within the class of tiling billiards in periodic tilings, is there a finite list of generic archetypal behaviors? In particular, does a general dichotomy hold in which typical trajectories are either dense in open sets or integrable in the sense of being periodic or linearly escaping?

evidence.quote: Two questions In the following, I ask two questions, related in spirit – one general and one specific. Question 1.In the class of tiling billiards in periodic tilings, is there a finite list of generic archetypal behaviors? For instance, the tiling billiards for which generic behavior is under- stood fall into one of two categories: either their typical trajectories are dense in open sets of the plane, as in cases 1. and 5., or the behavior is integrable (typical trajectories are either periodic or linearly escaping), as in cases 2. and 3. (and conjecturally in case 4. as well). One could ask whether such a dichotomy holds in general.

accepted.question_text: Describe the dynamics of generic trajectories in parallelogram tiling billiards, where the plane is tiled by parallelograms with side vectors a_vec = (a, 0) and b_vec = (b sin alpha, b cos alpha) and the dynamics depends on the parameters alpha in (0, pi) and m = a/b.

evidence.quote: Question 2.Describe the dynamics of (at least, generic) trajectories ofparallelogram tiling billiards, defined in a tiling of the plane by parallelograms with sides parallel to the vectors ⃗a:= (a,0)and ⃗b:= (bsinα, bcosα), wherea, b∈R + ∗ andα∈(0, π). The tiling billiard dynamics depends on two parameters,αandm= a b.

accepted.question_text: Provide examples of real-world systems from areas such as physics, mechanics, biology, or economics that can be modeled as locally conformal symplectic flows or maps.

evidence.quote: This more general setting could potentially be used to model a wider class of non- conservative systems with non-uniform or spatially dependent dissipation. However, the list of compelling real-world examples of systems that genuinely fit the framework remains surprisingly empty. Problem:Provide examples of real-world systems (from physics, mechanics, biology, eco- nomics, etc...) that can be modeled as locally conformal symplectic flows or maps.

evidence.quote: Putting together (10) and (11), we get the following equations of motion:  ˙x=∂ pH+ (p·˙x−H)¯η p ˙p=−∂ xH+f p−(p·˙x−H)¯η x. (12) Question:Is there a physical example that fits into the framework of equations(12)? What do equations(12)mean from a physical/mechanical point of view?

Hence, we could rewrite these equations as: 17 ===== PAGE 18 ===== Open problems in Billiards and Quantitative Symplectic Geometry  ˙x=∂ pH ˙p=−∂ xH+f p−L(x,˙x)¯η x. (14) Question:The termf pis what we were expecting (some dissipation/repulsion related to the momentum). However: what does the term−L(x,˙x)¯ηx represent? Does it have any

accepted.question_text: For a projective billiard, when does the system possess an invariant symplectic form, and when does it admit a variational description?

evidence.quote: Serge Tabachnikov Projective billiards.Projective billiards were defined in [107]. A projective billiard table is a convex domain with smooth boundary equipped with a transverse field of directions; the phase space is the same as that of the conventional billiard, and the reflection law is such that the tangential component of the incoming velocity vector is preserved, whereas the transverse component is reversed. Problem:when does a projective billiard possess an invariant symplectic form?And, related, when does it have a variational description?

accepted.question_text: Given a closed immersed submanifold M of Euclidean space, estimate from below the number of generalized n-periodic billiard trajectories, meaning inscribed n-gons with vertices on M that have extremal perimeter, in terms of the topology of M and the immersion.

evidence.quote: Inner and outer periodic orbits on immersed submanifolds.LetMbe a closed immersed submanifold of Euclidean space. A generalizedn-periodic billiard trajectory is an n-gon inscribed inM(that is, the vertices lie onM) that has an extremal perimeter. Problem:estimate from below the number ofn-periodic trajectories in terms of the topology ofMand the immersion.

accepted.question_text: Study the complexity function of polygonal symplectic billiards, where the complexity p(n) counts the number of length-n subwords arising from symbolic codings of billiard trajectories by polygon sides.

evidence.quote: Complexity of polygonal symplectic billiards.Symplectic billiards were introduced in [10]; the polygonal ones were studied in [4, 8]. Label the sides of a polygon by some symbols and assign to a billiard trajectory its symbolic code, the corresponding bi-infinite word in these symbols. The set of all trajectories yields a collection of such words. The complexity functionp(n)is the number of different subwords of lengthnin this collection. For the conventional polygonal billiards, it is known that the complexity function is sub- exponential (the literature on this subject is huge, starting with [82]), and it has polynomial, actually cubic, growth for rational billiards (whose angles are rational multiples ofπ). For polygonal outer billiards, the growth of the complexity function is polynomial, see [67]. Problem:study the complexity of polygonal symplectic billiards.

accepted.question_text: Do there exist chaotic symplectic billiards in convex C^1-smooth domains?

evidence.quote: Chaotic symplectic billiards?Problem:do chaotic symplectic billiards in convexC 1- smooth domains exist? A natural candidate is the stadium; there is numerical evidence that this might be the case.

accepted.question_text: How restrictive is the condition that the characteristic curves on a smooth hypersurface in standard symplectic space are planar?

evidence.quote: Planar characteristic curves.LetMbe (a germ of) a smooth hypersurface in the standard symplectic space. Problem:how restrictive is the condition that the characteristic curves onMare planar?

accepted.question_text: In a domain with smooth boundary in Euclidean space, fix a generic interior point P and consider the time-t front consisting of light rays emitted from P in all directions, reflected specularly at the boundary, and evolved for time t. Does this time-t front become dense as t tends to infinity?

evidence.quote: Density of reflected wave fronts.Consider a domain with smooth boundary in Eu- clidean space and letPbe a generic point in its interior. LetPbe a source of light, and consider the timet-front, that is, the locus of “photons" emanating fromPin all directions, undergoing optical reflection off the boundary, and traveling for timet. Problem:does this timet-front become dense in the limitt→ ∞?

accepted.question_text: Prove Ivrii's conjecture for 3-periodic multidimensional outer symplectic billiards with respect to a strictly convex closed hypersurface.

evidence.quote: Ivrii’sconjectureforoutersymplecticbilliards.TheIvriiconjectureforconventional billiards asserts that the set of periodic orbits has zero measure (with respect to the measure associated with the invariant symplectic structure of the phase space). A similar conjecture applies to the outer symplectic billiards. For conventional billiards, this conjecture is proved for periodsn= 2,3in all dimensions, and forn= 4in dimension 2. For planar outer billiards, there are proofs for2n+ 1-periodic orbits with rotation numbernand for2n-periodic orbits with rotation numbern−1. See [102] and the references therein about these results. Problem:prove Ivrii’s conjecture for 3-periodic multidimensional outer symplectic billiards with respect to a strictly convex closed hypersurface.

accepted.question_text: When a smooth convex closed curve is approximated by n-gons and the quality of approximation is measured by the area of the symmetric difference, is the resulting function of n related to some billiard-like dynamical system?

evidence.quote: Approximating a convex curve by polygons with respect to the symmetric differ- ence.A popular topic in convex geometry is the approximation of smooth convex closed curves by polygons, see [63]. The quality of approximation byn-gons may be measured by the difference of perimeters or areas, and the polygons may be inscribed or circumscribed. This leads to four functions ofn, and these four functions correspond to the minimal ac- tions of four billiards: the conventional, Birkhoff, billiards, the outer billiards, the symplectic billiards, and the outer length billiards (see [11, 24] about these lesser studied billiards). Consider the approximation of an oval byn-gons where the quality of approximation is measured by the area of the symmetric difference. Problem:is the resulting function ofnrelated to some billiard-like dynamical system?

accepted.question_text: How can one characterize the optical symplectic transformations of the space of oriented lines, namely the local symplectic transformations realizable by finite collections of mirrors, inside the full space of symplectic transformations of the space of oriented lines?

evidence.quote: Optical transformations of the space of oriented lines.The billiard (optical) reflec- tion is a (local) symplectic transformation of the space of oriented lines (rays of light); this space carries a symplectic structure induced by the canonical symplectic structure of the cotangent bundle of the ambientn-dimensional space via symplectic reduction. The space of oriented lines has dimension2n−2, and its symplectic transformation de- pends on a smooth function of2n−2variables (for example, an infinitesimal symplectic transformation, i.e., a Hamiltonian vector field, is defined by its Hamiltonian function). On the other hand, a smooth mirror is a hypersurface, locally the graph of a function of n−1variables. Such functions constitute a minuscule part of the space of smooth functions of2n−2variables. Thus, one expects theopticalset of (local) symplectic transformations of the space of rays that are realized by a finite collection of mirrors to be a very small part of the set of all (local) symplectic transformations of the space of rays. 19 ===== PAGE 20 ===== Open problems in Billiards and Quantitative Symplectic Geometry Problem:how to characterize this optical symplectic transformation within the space of all symplectic transformations of the space of oriented lines?
